## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  Curiosity-driven benchmark, awkwardly placed in this heat by deliberate
  choice. May migrate out when ₣A_ splits. Goal is data, not a binding
  decision — no code lands by default; results inform whether to spend a
  follow-up changing pipeline `machineType`.
  
  ## Motivation
  
  Build wall-clock has drifted out of memory. Cloud Build defaults to a
  small machine type; working assumption has been that conjure is
  network/IO-bound (base-image pulls, apt fetches, layer pushes) and
  bigger CPU would not help materially. Want a number, not a hunch.
  
  ## What
  
  Run the same conjure pipeline twice on the same vessel, varying ONLY
  `options.machineType`:
  
    - Run 1: current default (no `machineType` set)
    - Run 2: `E2_HIGHCPU_8`
  
  Optional Run 3: `E2_HIGHCPU_32` if Run 2 shows clear linear scaling.
  
  Same vessel both runs — pick one of tadmor / srjcl / pluml (rebuilt
  anyway by ₢A_AAF immediately prior, so the cache state is comparable).
  
  ## How
  
    - Identify the Cloud Build step config touched by conjure (RBSAC
      territory; RBSAB about-pipeline may also be a candidate target).
    - Add `options: { machineType: E2_HIGHCPU_8 }` to a one-off variant
      invocation; do NOT commit it to the canonical pipeline.
    - Capture wall-clock from `gcloud builds list` / build log
      timestamps for each run; record per-step durations if surfaced.
    - Compare.
  
  ## Outcome
  
  A short note recorded back to this docket (or paddock) with:
  
    - Run 1 wall-clock and per-step breakdown
    - Run 2 wall-clock and per-step breakdown
    - Verdict: CPU-bound, IO-bound, or mixed
    - Recommendation: nominate a follow-up heat to change `machineType`,
      itch it for later, or close the question.
  
  No production-config changes from this pace. Discoveries that would
  change the canonical pipeline get filed as separate heats / itches.
  
  ## Adjacency note
  
  Sits between ₢A_AAF (complete test suite — rebuilds tadmor/srjcl/pluml
  against fresh depot) and ₢A_AAG (onboarding tracks). AAF provides the
  cache-warm baseline; piggybacking minimizes incremental Cloud Build
  spend.


Recommended: `jjx_arm` ₢₢A_AAZ or mount A_

# jjezs_paddock ₣A_

## Context

Three interwoven infrastructure cleanups culminating in a depot regeneration:

1. **Resource prefixing** (`₢A_AAA`/`₢A_AAB`/`₢A_AAC`) — introduce `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` (cloud-visible names: GAR, Cloud Build, GCP) and `RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX` (local container and network names). Enables multi-regime isolation on shared infrastructure.

2. **GAR categorical layout migration** (`₢A_AAK`/`₢A_AAL`) — reorganize GAR top-level namespace into three prefixed categories. Dissolves the `<vessel>:<hallmark>-<suffix>` hyphen-suffix scheme and the `vouches/` aggregator. Tabtargets taking `(vessel, hallmark)` simplify to `(hallmark)`.

3. **Payor credential subdirectory** (`₢A_AAD`) — move `rbro.env` into a `payor/` subdirectory to match the role-subdirectory pattern already established for RBRA credentials.

The depot regen at the tail (`₢A_AAE`) is the hard cutover point. All three threads land before it; depot regen validates the new infrastructure in regenerated form.

## Design decisions captured

### GAR categorical layout

Three top-level namespaces under the cloud prefix:

```
<prefix>hallmarks/<hallmark>/{image, vouch, pouch, about, attest, diags}
<prefix>reliquaries/<date>/<tool>
<prefix>enshrines/<base>
```

Each immediate child of `<prefix>hallmarks` IS a hallmark; the six ark types become plain basename siblings. No more vessel-in-path or suffix-on-tag string grammar.

### Vessel identity

No longer lives in the GAR path. Resolved on demand from the `vouch` or `about` metadata of the hallmark in question. `ls <prefix>hallmarks/` no longer surfaces vessel at a glance; tally and plumb already read metadata for health, and extend the pattern where operation prose needs the vessel name.

### Attest vs vouch

Both persist as peer basenames under `<hallmark>/`. Attestations advertise during build for SLSA provenance; vouches check them at consumption. Load-bearing split — retained by design.

### Pouch

A `FROM scratch` OCI image carrying build context (Dockerfile + supporting files) to Cloud Build's first step. Present for conjure/bind, absent for graft (graft is local push — no GCB involved). Now lives at `<prefix>hallmarks/<hallmark>/pouch` in the new layout.

### SUFFIX → BASENAME constant rename

`RBGC_ARK_SUFFIX_*` constants persist but rename to `RBGC_ARK_BASENAME_*`; their values drop the leading hyphen. Role shifts from "suffix to hyphen-concat" to "literal basename of a sibling file." The concat grammar disappears entirely from ark path construction.

### `vouches/` aggregator removal

`RBGC_VOUCHES_PACKAGE` existed to enable hallmark-only vouch lookup without knowing the vessel. The new layout delivers the same property naturally — every hallmark has its own `vouch` basename under `<prefix>hallmarks/<hallmark>/`. The aggregator becomes redundant and is deleted.

### Cutover discipline

Hard cutover — old and new path shapes do not coexist in live code. Depot regen at `₢A_AAE` is the forcing function; all layout work lands before it.

## References

- `₢A_AAJ` — filesystem-side config-directory naming research (orthogonal to this heat). Decisions recorded in that pace: `rbmm_moorings` umbrella, `rbm*_` prefix family, hard-cutover at depot regen. Filesystem rename itself flows to a separate follow-up heat and is out of this heat's scope.
- `Tools/rbk/rbgc_Constants.sh:122-131` — current ark-suffix constants and `RBGC_VOUCHES_PACKAGE`; this heat renames to `_BASENAME_*` and removes the aggregator.
- `Tools/rbk/rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh:712-733` — pouch definition and current concat-based path construction.
- `Tools/rbk/rbfl_FoundryLedger.sh:440-650` — current per-suffix HEAD/DELETE dance; collapses to subtree operations in `₢A_AAK`.

# jjezs_pace ₢A_AAZ

## Character

Curiosity-driven benchmark, awkwardly placed in this heat by deliberate
choice. May migrate out when ₣A_ splits. Goal is data, not a binding
decision — no code lands by default; results inform whether to spend a
follow-up changing pipeline `machineType`.

## Motivation

Build wall-clock has drifted out of memory. Cloud Build defaults to a
small machine type; working assumption has been that conjure is
network/IO-bound (base-image pulls, apt fetches, layer pushes) and
bigger CPU would not help materially. Want a number, not a hunch.

## What

Run the same conjure pipeline twice on the same vessel, varying ONLY
`options.machineType`:

  - Run 1: current default (no `machineType` set)
  - Run 2: `E2_HIGHCPU_8`

Optional Run 3: `E2_HIGHCPU_32` if Run 2 shows clear linear scaling.

Same vessel both runs — pick one of tadmor / srjcl / pluml (rebuilt
anyway by ₢A_AAF immediately prior, so the cache state is comparable).

## How

  - Identify the Cloud Build step config touched by conjure (RBSAC
    territory; RBSAB about-pipeline may also be a candidate target).
  - Add `options: { machineType: E2_HIGHCPU_8 }` to a one-off variant
    invocation; do NOT commit it to the canonical pipeline.
  - Capture wall-clock from `gcloud builds list` / build log
    timestamps for each run; record per-step durations if surfaced.
  - Compare.

## Outcome

A short note recorded back to this docket (or paddock) with:

  - Run 1 wall-clock and per-step breakdown
  - Run 2 wall-clock and per-step breakdown
  - Verdict: CPU-bound, IO-bound, or mixed
  - Recommendation: nominate a follow-up heat to change `machineType`,
    itch it for later, or close the question.

No production-config changes from this pace. Discoveries that would
change the canonical pipeline get filed as separate heats / itches.

## Adjacency note

## ₢A_AAZ — cloudbuild-machinetype-ab-bench

**Pace goal:** Run a 2-run A/B benchmark of Cloud Build's `options.machineType` (default vs `E2_HIGHCPU_8`) against one conjure pipeline to determine whether conjure is CPU-bound or IO-bound, and produce a recommendation (follow-up heat, itch, or close the question). **No production config changes from this pace.**

content.text:
Right — tadmor went bind/graft, leaving moriah and srjcl/pluml as the conjure-mode candidates. Moriah it is.

**Proposed reslate shape for ₢A_AAZ** — sketching what the new docket needs to capture before we write it. Treat this as a starting point to iterate on:

### What the experiment looks like

For each `machineType` condition (default, `E2_HIGHCPU_8`, optionally `E2_HIGHCPU_32`):
1. Run N consecutive moriah bottle conjures with the same machineType.
2. Discard the first build's timing (cache priming).
3. Take steady-state wall-clock from runs 2..N (median, or all reported).

Move to next machineType, repeat. Final report compares steady-state numbers.

### Open design dials (need your call on each)

1. **N per condition.** 3 builds gives 1 prime + 2 measurements; 4 gives 1 prime + 3. With ~5 min per build, 4 conditions × N = significant Cloud Build spend.
   - 3 conditions × 4 builds = 12 builds (~60 min, ~$$)
   - 2 conditions × 3 builds = 6 builds
2. **What gets benchmarked.** Bottle (`rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap`) only? Or also sentry (`rbev-sentry-deb-tether`)? Or include the about-pipeline (RBSAB) which conjure invokes alongside the main image? Different stages may show different CPU/IO mixes.
3. **Cache mechanism understanding.** Need to confirm what "cache priming" actually warms — is it `--cache-from` against GAR, Cloud Build's worker-pool layer cache, or both? This affects whether builds 2..N actually share a warm cache. (Investigation step; can be answered before runs start.)
4. **machineType override mechanism.** The docket says "do not commit to canonical pipeline." Practical options:
   - Environment variable wired into the cloudbuild yaml `${_MACHINE_TYPE}` substitution (if substitutions are available)
   - Local-only edit of the yaml, run, revert, run again — risk of leaking into a commit
   - One-shot `gcloud builds submit --machine-type=...` invocation that bypasses the pipeline's `options` block (cleanest if it works)
5. **Capture surface.** `gcloud builds list` wall-clock vs. per-step durations from `gcloud builds log <id>` vs. structured fields from `gcloud builds describe`. Per-step is the gold for diagnosing CPU vs. IO, wall-clock alone for headline.
6. **Where results land.** Reslated docket itself (keeps the result with the question), paddock under a Results section, or a dedicated memo like `Memos/memo-YYYYMMDD-cloudbuild-machinetype-bench.md`.
7. **Cleanup posture.** Abjure each iteration's hallmark from GAR between runs, or let them accumulate and clean up at end? Doesn't affect timing; affects depot tidiness.

### Out-of-scope clarifications to add

- No production pipeline changes from this pace (already in docket — keep).
- Update vessel-pool rationale: drop the tadmor mention, name moriah and reasoning (tadmor is now bind, srjcl/pluml are conjure but moriah is the AAF-out-of-scope pick).
- Note that AAF's piggyback rationale no longer applies; explicit warmup is part of the design.

### My recommendations on the open dials

- N=3, 2 conditions (default + E2_HIGHCPU_8). 6 builds total. Add E2_HIGHCPU_32 as conditional Run 7-9 only if HIGHCPU_8 shows clear scaling.
- Bottle only — keeps the pipeline-under-test fixed. About-pipeline likely runs same machineType anyway; if you want about separately, that's a different pace.
- Override via `gcloud builds submit --machine-type=` if conjure's invocation surface permits; otherwise env-var substitution. Investigate first.
- Per-step durations captured for each build. Wall-clock for headline.
- Results land in the docket; if the answer is "spend a follow-up," that follow-up gets a memo with the data.

Which of these need different calls? Once we agree on the dials I'll write the gazette and reslate.

And `rbgp_Payor.sh:803-810` shows the depot creates two private pools at levy time — one for tether builds, one for airgap. Moriah's bottle (`RBRV_EGRESS_MODE=airgap`) is hard-wired to the airgap pool. Both pools' machineType reads from `RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE`.

content.text:
**Confirmed: yes, the airgap private pool must be recreated to change machineType.** Conjure submits to `RBDC_POOL_AIRGAP` for airgap vessels (`rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh:704`), and vouch does the same (`rbfv_FoundryVerify.sh:336`). The pool's machineType is set at pool creation from `RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE` (`rbgp_Payor.sh:780-787`). No per-build override available with private pools.

**Enshrine concern, addressed:** Enshrine (`rbw-dE`) is a separate one-time Cloud Build that copies the upstream base into GAR. It runs *before* conjure. Conjure pulls FROM the enshrined image, not from Docker Hub. So:
- Enshrine timing does *not* enter per-iteration conjure timing.
- Moriah's base does need to be enshrined on 100003 before any benchmark (it was out of AAF's scope, so it hasn't been done yet on the new depot).
- One-time setup cost; doesn't contaminate the A/B.

**But the worker-pool finding kills the interleaved design.** Each switch between default and high-CPU requires destroying and recreating the airgap pool — minutes of operational work per switch, and the recreation itself introduces variability. Three structural paths:

| Path | How | Trade-offs |
|------|-----|------------|
| **A. Full depot regen per condition** | `RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE`=X, `rbw-dL`, run builds; tear down, regen with new value | Cleanest, slowest. Each condition is a fresh depot. Piggy-back-able on already-planned regen cycles. |
| **B. Pool-only recreate per condition** | Targeted delete + create of just the airgap pool. Likely needs a small ad-hoc script (no tabtarget exists). | Faster than full regen. Snowflake risk — operating outside the depot lifecycle. |
| **C. Bypass private pool, use shared pool** | Modify conjure to submit to Google's default pool. Per-build `options.machineType` then works. | Cheap and properly interleavable. But breaks moriah's airgap network restriction — defeats the airgap defining characteristic. May or may not matter for a CPU-vs-IO benchmark. |

**Reduced interleaving consequence.** With pool-bound machineType, the practical design becomes a *block* design: e.g.,
- Pool at default: build 1 (warmup, discard) → build 2 (measure) → build 3 (measure)
- Recreate pool at high-CPU equivalent: build 4 (warmup, discard) → build 5 (measure) → build 6 (measure)

6 conjure builds + 6 vouch builds + 1 enshrine setup. Time-of-day noise becomes a confound (mitigated only by running back-to-back in the same hour).

If you want the interleaved 5-build design you originally proposed, you'd need path C (shared pool, accepting loss of airgap fidelity) — that's a meaningful experimental-cleanliness gain at the cost of testing-a-different-pipeline.

**My read:** Path A piggybacked on an imminent depot regen is the lowest-friction. Each regen flips `RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE`, run conjure 2-3 times, capture, done. The 5-build interleaved design is incompatible with private pools regardless.

Which path do you want the reslated docket to spec? And — do you want me to write the gazette now, or keep discussing?

content.text:
**Vouch is even more locked-in — it always uses POOL_AIRGAP regardless of vessel egress mode** (`rbfv_FoundryVerify.sh:336, 533, 763`). So a tether vessel's full iteration touches both pools: conjure on POOL_TETHER, vouch on POOL_AIRGAP. Both private, both bound to the same `RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE`.

**Summary of escape routes from the depot-rebuild constraint:**

1. **Pool selection — no help.** Tether and airgap both private. Vouch is hardwired to airgap.
2. **Code escape — possible but invasive.** Edit `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh:702-706` and `rbfv_FoundryVerify.sh:336/533/763` to drop the `pool` field from the build JSON, falling back to Google's default shared pool where `options.machineType` works per-build. Three vouch sites + one conjure site = 4 edits. Throwaway branch. Loses airgap *network* fidelity (default pool has internet), but if the experiment is about CPU/IO scaling, the network restriction is probably orthogonal to what we're measuring.
3. **Pool-recreate without depot rebuild — possible.** Cherry-pick the pool-create logic from `rbgp_Payor.sh:780-810` into a one-off script. Delete pool via gcloud, recreate with new machineType, no depot churn. Likely the cleanest middle path if you want to preserve airgap fidelity.
4. **Full depot regen per condition.** Heaviest, cleanest, piggybacks on planned regen cycles.

**Net answer to your question: no, changing vessel choice does not let us avoid touching the pool.** The constraint lives one level up — at the depot/pool layer — and any conjure-mode vessel inherits it.

Worth noting: this finding may itself be valuable, independent of the benchmark — it documents that `RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE` is a *depot-time* setting, not a *build-time* knob. That structural fact could land in the paddock or as a memo regardless of which path we take.

Want me to look at path 3 (write a small pool-recreate script) and path 2 (audit the code-edit footprint) in more detail before you choose?

**Honest answer: no single fixture does depot-levy + single conjure end-to-end.** The closest pieces:

| Fixture | What it does | What it skips |
|---------|-------------|---------------|
| `pristine-lifecycle` | Depot levy → SA cycle → live-disqualify → depot teardown | No reliquary, no enshrine, no conjure |
| `canonical-establish` | Depot levy → governor/retriever/director invest | Stops at SAs ready — no reliquary, no enshrine, no conjure |
| `hallmark-lifecycle` | Single `rbev-busybox` conjure ordain → audit → abjure → audit | Assumes depot + SAs + reliquary already exist |
| `onboarding-sequence` | inscribe-reliquary → kludge-tadmor/ccyolo → 4× ordain (conjure/airgap/bind/graft) | Assumes depot + SAs already exist |

To get "fresh depot through single Cloud Build," you'd manually chain: `canonical-establish` → `rbw-dI` (inscribe reliquary) → `rbw-dE` (enshrine the target vessel's base) → `hallmark-lifecycle`. No tabtarget chains them.

**Two structural issues worth flagging for the reslate:**

1. **`hallmark-lifecycle` uses `rbev-busybox`** — chosen for fast tests precisely because it's tiny. From `rbtdrc_crucible.rs:2557`: *"rbev-busybox is the load-bearing vessel — small, fast, conjure-mode."* If we benchmark with busybox, the conjure is so trivial that CPU-vs-IO differences may not surface at all. The benchmark signal you want comes from a vessel with real build work — moriah's `rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap` (Rust binary, multi-platform) or srjcl's jupyter image. The fixture's vessel choice is wrong for *this* question.

2. **The depot teardown belongs to a different fixture** (`pristine-lifecycle` case 5). To loop "rebuild depot → conjure → tear down → change machineType → repeat," there's no orchestration that does the whole loop — it's manual or scripted.

**Implication for the reslate:** the benchmark protocol is going to be hand-orchestrated regardless of fixture availability. Either:

- **Option α**: Manual chain via existing tabtargets (`rbw-dL`, `rbw-aM`, `rbw-arI`, `rbw-adI`, `rbw-dI`, `rbw-dE`, `rbw-fO`, `rbw-dU`) parameterized by an `RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE` change between iterations. Heaviest vessel choice (moriah's ifrit-airgap) gives best signal. Each iteration is ~20-30 min of operator-driven steps.

- **Option β**: Mint a benchmark-specific fixture that chains the steps, takes `RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE` as a parameter, runs N conjures, tears down. Heavier investment but reusable. Probably scope-creep for ₢A_AAZ which is supposed to be a curiosity-driven benchmark, not infrastructure work.

- **Option γ**: Accept busybox as the vessel-under-test and run `canonical-establish` + manual reliquary/enshrine + `hallmark-lifecycle`, repeating. Lightest workflow but the signal may be too weak to answer the question.

**My recommendation for the reslate:** Option α (manual chain, moriah's ifrit-airgap vessel) — the docket's whole character is "curiosity-driven, deliberately awkward placement, may migrate out." Hand-orchestrated fits that character. Reslated docket should spell out the per-iteration step sequence and what to capture.

Want me to draft the reslated docket along those lines?

input.content:
# jjezs_reslate A_AAZ

## Character

Curiosity-driven benchmark, hand-orchestrated. May migrate out when ₣A_
splits. Goal is data, not a binding decision — no production config
changes by default. Single deliverable: a memo with findings and a
recommendation (follow-up heat, itch, or close).

## Motivation

Build wall-clock has drifted out of memory. Want a number on whether
bigger CPU helps conjure, or whether the pipeline is network/IO-bound.

Slating investigation surfaced the load-bearing constraint:
`RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE` is a *depot-time* setting baked into private
worker pools at levy. Both pools (tether and airgap) inherit it.
Conjure submits to the egress-mode-matched pool; vouch is hardwired to
the airgap pool. No per-build `options.machineType` override path
exists. Each machineType condition therefore requires its own depot
lifecycle (or, at minimum, pool teardown-and-recreate — no tabtarget
for that today).

This structural fact is independently useful — capturing it in the
output memo earns the pace its keep even if the timing data is
inconclusive.

## What

Vessel under test: moriah's bottle (`rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap`).
Heavier than `rbev-busybox`, conjure-mode, multi-platform Rust binary
— enough real build work to surface CPU-vs-IO signal. Sentry vessel
out of scope. Tadmor no longer viable (moved to bind/graft).

Two depot lifecycles, one per machineType condition:
  - Condition A: current default `RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE` (record the
    value at start of pace).
  - Condition B: upgraded Compute Engine type — `e2-standard-8`,
    `n2d-standard-8`, or `c3-standard-8`. Pick at mount-time.
  - Optional Condition C: further-upgraded value if B shows clear
    scaling worth extending the curve.

## How — per condition

  1. Edit `RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE` in `rbrr.env` to the target value.
